questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: The largest number that divides $70$ and $125$, leaving remainders $5$ and $8$ respectively, is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Subtract the remainders, then take the HCF.

**Working.** $70-5=65$, $125-8=117$; HCF$(65,117)=13$.

**Answer.** $13$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: If CAT is coded as DBU, then DOG is coded as
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each letter shifts forward by one position.

**Working.** D→E, O→P, G→H gives EPH.

**Answer.** EPH.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: Identify the grammatically correct sentence (free of a dangling modifier).
options: Walking through the tunnel, the engineer was protected by the helmet from falling debris.
Walking through the tunnel, the helmet protected the engineer from falling debris.
The helmet, walking through the tunnel, protected the engineer.
Walking through the tunnel, falling debris was stopped by the helmet.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The introductory '-ing' phrase must modify the subject that immediately follows it.

**Working.** Only the engineer can 'walk through the tunnel'; the helmet and the debris cannot. Hence the subject after the comma must be 'the engineer'.

**Answer.** 'Walking through the tunnel, the engineer was protected by the helmet ...'.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: The lowercase letter shown, rotated $180^\circ$ in the plane, looks like the letter

figure.caption: The lowercase letter b.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 120 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><text x='35' y='88' font-size='80' fill='#334155'>b</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** A $180^\circ$ rotation flips both top-bottom and left-right.

**Working.** 'b' rotated $180^\circ$ becomes 'q'.

**Answer.** q.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: To find a man's age, consider: (I) He is $5$ years older than his brother. (II) His brother is $20$ years old. Which is sufficient?
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Identify the minimal information that fixes a unique value.

**Working.** I gives a relation, II gives the brother's age; together the man is $25$. Neither alone suffices.

**Answer.** Both I and II together.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: The volume of a cube of side $4$ is
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume of a cube $=\text{side}^3$.

**Working.** $4^3=64$.

**Answer.** $64$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: The remainder when $2^{10}$ is divided by $7$ is _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Use $2^3=8\equiv 1 \pmod 7$.

**Working.** $2^{10}=(2^3)^3\cdot 2 \equiv 1^3\cdot 2 = 2 \pmod 7$.

**Answer.** $2$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.topic: Spatial Aptitude — Paper Folding and Punching
questions.stem: A square sheet is folded three times (into one-eighth) and one hole is punched through all layers. When unfolded, the number of holes is _____ .

figure.caption: A square folded three times into an eighth with one hole punched.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 100 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><rect x='30' y='25' width='30' height='60' fill='rgba(244,63,94,0.10)' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='45' cy='55' r='6' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Three folds create $2^3 = 8$ layers.

**Working.** One punch through $8$ layers gives $8$ holes.

**Answer.** $8$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.topic: Spatial Aptitude — Paper Folding and Punching
questions.stem: A square sheet is folded once in half and three holes are punched through both layers. When unfolded, the total number of holes is _____ .

figure.caption: A square folded in half with three holes punched.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 160 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><rect x='20' y='20' width='60' height='70' fill='rgba(99,102,241,0.10)' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='20' x2='80' y2='90' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><circle cx='45' cy='38' r='5' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='45' cy='55' r='5' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='45' cy='72' r='5' fill='none' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Holes $=$ punches $\times$ layers.

**Working.** $1$ fold gives $2$ layers; $3$ punches make $3\times 2 = 6$ holes.

**Answer.** $6$.

questions.subject: General Aptitude
questions.stem: From the same sales data ($40, 60, 30, 80$ units over four years), the average annual sales is _____ units.

figure.caption: Bar chart of annual sales for 2018-2021: 40, 60, 30, 80 units.
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img'><line x1='30' y1='160' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='40' y='100' width='28' height='60' fill='#6366f1'/><rect x='80' y='70' width='28' height='90' fill='#6366f1'/><rect x='120' y='115' width='28' height='45' fill='#6366f1'/><rect x='160' y='40' width='28' height='120' fill='#6366f1'/><line x1='30' y1='82.5' x2='220' y2='82.5' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5 3'/><text x='150' y='78' font-size='10' fill='#ef4444'>average</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Average = total / number of years.

**Working.** $\dfrac{40+60+30+80}{4}=\dfrac{210}{4}=52.5$.

**Answer.** $52.5$.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A total station measures a slope distance of 100.0 m at a vertical angle of 30°. The horizontal distance, in m, is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $=$ slope distance $\times\cos(\text{vertical angle})$.

**Working.**
$$H=100.0\cos30^{\circ}=100.0\times0.8660=86.6\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 86.6 m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A fold in rock strata is
options: a bend or flexure produced mainly by compressive forces
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Folds record ductile deformation.

**Reasoning.** Compressive (and sometimes shear) stresses buckle layered rocks into folds; the geometry (limbs, hinge, axial plane) describes their form.

**Answer.** A bend produced by compression.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In the levelling setup shown, the reduced level of A is 100.000 m, the back sight on A is 1.500 m and the fore sight on B is 2.300 m. The reduced level of B, in m, is _____ (three decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='10' y1='140' x2='250' y2='140' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='130' y1='70' x2='130' y2='90' stroke='#1f2937' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='68' r='5' fill='#475569'/><text x='116' y='60' font-size='9'>level</text><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='40' y2='140' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='84' font-size='9' fill='#dc2626'>A</text><text x='44' y='112' font-size='9'>BS 1.5</text><line x1='220' y1='80' x2='220' y2='140' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='212' y='74' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>B</text><text x='176' y='112' font-size='9'>FS 2.3</text><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/></svg>
figure.caption: Differential levelling A to B

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: In the section shown, tilted older strata are truncated by an erosion surface (dashed) and overlain by near-horizontal younger beds. This relationship is an example of an

figure.caption: Tilted beds truncated below sub-horizontal beds

questions.solution:
**Concept.** An angular unconformity shows a clear angular discordance across the break.

**Reasoning.** The older beds were tilted and eroded before the younger, gently dipping beds were deposited, so the bedding above and below the surface meet at an angle — the defining feature of an angular unconformity.

**Answer.** Angular unconformity.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A mineral specimen of mass 50 g displaces 20 cm³ of water. Its specific gravity is ____ (one decimal).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $SG=\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{mass of equal volume of water}}=\dfrac{m}{V\rho_w}$.

**Working.**
$$SG=\frac{50\ \text{g}}{20\ \text{cm}^3\times1\ \text{g/cm}^3}=2.5$$

**Answer.** 2.5.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A triangular plot has corner coordinates (0, 0), (40, 0) and (0, 30) m. Its area by the coordinate (cross-product) method, in m², is ____.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: Successive staff readings give a backsight of $2.0\ \text{m}$ and a foresight of $1.2\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the rise from the first point to the second (m).

figure.caption: Rise between readings
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='180' y2='40' stroke='#475569' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='40' y1='40' x2='40' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb'/><line x1='180' y1='40' x2='180' y2='100' stroke='#16a34a'/><text x='24' y='100' font-size='8'>BS 2.0</text><text x='158' y='90' font-size='8'>FS 1.2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Rise $=BS-FS$ (positive means rising ground).

**Working.**
$$\text{Rise}=2.0-1.2=0.8\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $0.8$ m.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: The fore bearing of a line is $70^\circ$ (whole-circle bearing). Compute its back bearing in degrees.

figure.caption: Fore and back bearing
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 180 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='40' y2='150' stroke='#cbd5e1'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='140' y2='55' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='-20' y2='111' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='25' font-size='8'>N</text><text x='90' y='62' font-size='8'>FB=70 deg</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** For a whole-circle fore bearing below $180^\circ$, back bearing $=$ FB $+180^\circ$.

**Working.**
$$\text{BB}=70^\circ+180^\circ=250^\circ$$

**Answer.** $250^\circ$.

questions.subject: mine-surveying
questions.stem: A traverse of perimeter $1000$ m has a linear closing error of $0.5$ m. Express the relative precision as a decimal fraction (error per unit length).

figure.caption: Traverse precision
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,130 100,30 180,70 140,130' fill='none' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2'/><text x='25' y='150' font-size='8'>perimeter=1000 m, e=0.5 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Relative precision $=$ closing error divided by total traverse length.

**Working.**
$$\text{precision}=\frac{e}{P}=\frac{0.5}{1000}=0.0005\ \left(=\tfrac{1}{2000}\right)$$

**Answer.** $0.0005$.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A $10\,\text{kg}$ block on a frictionless floor is pushed by a horizontal force of $30\,\text{N}$. Its acceleration is _____ m/s$^2$.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $a=F/m$ with no friction.

**Working.**
$$a=\frac{30}{10}=3.$$

**Answer.** $3\,\text{m/s}^2$.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: With K = 100, C = 0 and a horizontal staff intercept of 1.20 m, the horizontal distance, in m, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $D=Ks+C$.

**Working.**
$$D=100\times1.20=120\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 120 m.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock mass with RMR $=59$ falls in
questions.solution:
**Concept.** RMR classes in steps of 20: I (81-100), II (61-80), III (41-60), IV (21-40), V (<21).

**Working.** $59$ lies in 41-60.

**Answer.** Class III — fair rock.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: For an airway of area $12\,\text{m}^2$ and perimeter $14\,\text{m}$, the hydraulic mean depth $m=A/P$ is _____ m. Round to two decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $m=\dfrac{A}{P}$.

**Working.**
$$m=\frac{12}{14}=0.857.$$

**Answer.** $\approx 0.86\,\text{m}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: At a variable cost of $\textsf{Rs}\,60$/unit, the total variable cost for $10{,}000$ units is $\textsf{Rs}$ _____ .
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $TVC=VC\times\text{units}$.

**Working.**
$$60\times10000=600000.$$

**Answer.** $\textsf{Rs}\,6{,}00{,}000$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Percentage extraction during the development (first working) stage of bord-and-pillar is low because
options: the coal seam is very thin
most of the coal is left in pillars to support the roof
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Pillars carry the overburden load during development.

**Reasoning.** Only the gallery coal is won initially; the bulk stays in standing pillars to keep the roof up, so first-working extraction is typically 25–40%.

**Answer.** Most coal is locked in support pillars.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: For the diagonal matrix shown, find the largest eigenvalue.

figure.caption: Diagonal matrix
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><text x='25' y='52' font-size='16'>[</text><text x='45' y='45' font-size='12'>2   0</text><text x='45' y='70' font-size='12'>0   3</text><text x='98' y='52' font-size='16'>]</text><text x='25' y='100' font-size='9'>eigenvalues of diagonal matrix</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** The eigenvalues of a diagonal matrix are its diagonal entries.

**Working.**
$$\lambda_1=2,\quad\lambda_2=3\Rightarrow\lambda_{max}=3$$

**Answer.** $3$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: The figure shows a roadway in which a gas has accumulated as a layer against the roof, above the ventilating air stream. The gas most likely responsible is
options: methane (CH₄), which is lighter than air and collects at the roof

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='80' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='18' fill='#fca5a5'/><text x='120' y='44' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#991b1b'>gas layer</text><line x1='40' y1='80' x2='200' y2='80' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#afn)'/><defs><marker id='afn' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#2563eb'/></marker></defs><text x='110' y='98' font-size='9' fill='#2563eb'>airflow</text></svg>
figure.caption: Gas layered at the roof of a roadway

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Roof layering indicates a gas lighter than air.

**Reasoning.** Methane has a specific gravity of about 0.55, so it rises and forms layers along the roof where ventilation is weak; CO₂ and H₂S are denser than air and tend to collect near the floor.

**Answer.** Methane (CH₄).

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: The contour interval is the
options: horizontal distance between two adjacent contours
constant vertical distance (difference in elevation) between successive contours
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Interval is a vertical quantity.

**Reasoning.** It is the fixed elevation difference between consecutive contours (e.g. 1 m, 5 m); smaller intervals give more detail. The horizontal spacing is the 'horizontal equivalent'.

**Answer.** Vertical distance between contours.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Joints differ from faults in that joints are fractures
options: along which large displacement has occurred
along which there is little or no appreciable relative displacement
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Joints are fractures without slip.

**Reasoning.** They form by tension, cooling or unloading; though they show negligible movement, joint sets strongly control rock-mass strength, permeability, blasting and slope stability.

**Answer.** Fractures with negligible displacement.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: Observing a theodolite angle on both 'face left' and 'face right' and meaning the results
options: doubles the work for no benefit
eliminates instrumental errors such as line-of-collimation and trunnion-axis errors
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Reversing the telescope cancels symmetric errors.

**Reasoning.** Changing face reverses the sign of collimation and trunnion-axis (and horizontal-axis) errors, so their mean is free of these instrumental defects.

**Answer.** Eliminates instrumental errors.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: Three airways, each of resistance $9\,\text{Ns}^2/\text{m}^8$, are in parallel. The equivalent resistance is _____ Ns$^2$/m$^8$.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** For $n$ equal resistances in parallel, $R_{eq}=R/n^2$.

**Working.**
$$\frac{9}{3^2}=\frac{9}{9}=1.$$

**Answer.** $1\,\text{Ns}^2/\text{m}^8$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Granite is a
options: coarse-grained acidic (felsic) plutonic rock composed mainly of quartz, feldspar and mica
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Granite is the type felsic plutonic rock.

**Reasoning.** It crystallises slowly at depth, is rich in silica (quartz + alkali feldspar + mica), light-coloured and coarse-grained; widely used as dimension stone.

**Answer.** Coarse-grained acidic plutonic rock.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A key advantage of longwall over bord-and-pillar mining is
options: very high percentage extraction (80–90%) with concentrated, high output
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Longwall maximises recovery.

**Reasoning.** Because pillars are not left in the panel, recovery reaches 80–90% with high, concentrated production from a single face — at the cost of high capital equipment and planned surface subsidence.

**Answer.** High percentage extraction and concentrated output.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The fold shown has limbs dipping away from the crest (figure). It is:
options: an anticline, with the oldest beds in the core and limbs dipping away from the axis
a syncline, with the youngest beds in the core
a monocline with a single flexure
a recumbent fold with a horizontal axial plane

figure.caption: Arched fold
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M20 110 Q110 30 200 110' fill='none' stroke='#a16207' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M20 125 Q110 50 200 125' fill='none' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='3'/><text x='72' y='132' font-size='8'>beds arch upward</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fold nomenclature.

**Reasoning.** Limbs dipping away from a crest, with the oldest strata exposed in the core, define an anticline.

**Answer.** An anticline, with the oldest beds in the core and limbs dipping away from the axis.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: A total station measures a slope distance of 100 m to a target at a vertical angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal (see figure). The horizontal distance, in m, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='210' y2='120' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='30' y1='120' x2='190' y2='40' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><text x='92' y='72' font-size='10' fill='#2563eb'>L = 100 m</text><path d='M72 120 A42 42 0 0 0 58 100' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626'/><text x='76' y='113' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>30°</text><line x1='190' y1='40' x2='190' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/></svg>
figure.caption: Slope distance and vertical angle

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Horizontal distance $H=L\cos\theta$.

**Working.**
$$H=100\cos30^\circ=100\times0.8660=86.60\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** $86.60$ m.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Spontaneous heating (spontaneous combustion) of coal is initiated by
options: the exothermic low-temperature oxidation of coal, whose heat accumulates when it cannot be dissipated
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Coal slowly oxidises and releases heat.

**Reasoning.** Oxygen reacts with exposed coal exothermically; if ventilation supplies oxygen but cannot carry the heat away, the temperature climbs progressively to ignition — spontaneous combustion.

**Answer.** Self-heating from exothermic coal oxidation.

questions.subject: Engineering Mathematics
questions.stem: Using $\iiint_V dV$, the volume of a sphere of radius $2$ is _____ (round to 2 decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume of a sphere $=\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ (the triple integral of $1$ over the ball).

**Working.**
$$V=\tfrac{4}{3}\pi(2)^{3}=\tfrac{32}{3}\pi\approx 33.51$$

**Answer.** $\approx 33.51$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Acid mine drainage (AMD) is generated when
options: pyrite (FeS₂) in the rock is oxidised in the presence of water and air, producing sulphuric acid and dissolved metals
methane is drained from a coal seam
diesel exhaust mixes with mine air
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Sulphide oxidation acidifies mine water.

**Reasoning.** Exposure of pyrite to oxygen and water yields sulphuric acid and ferrous/ferric sulphate, dropping the pH and mobilising heavy metals — the origin of acidic, metal-laden mine discharges.

**Answer.** Oxidation of pyrite by air and water.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a line of levels the sum of all backsights is 3.250 m and the sum of all foresights is 2.100 m. The difference between the last and first reduced levels (last − first), in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Arithmetic check: $\sum BS-\sum FS=RL_{last}-RL_{first}$.

**Working.**
$$3.250-2.100=1.150\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** +1.15 m (net rise).

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The tonnage factor relates
options: volume of rock to its tonnage (via bulk density)
questions.solution:
**Concept.** It is the bulk density (or its reciprocal).

**Working.** Tonnage $=$ volume $\div$ tonnage factor (or $\times$ density).

**Answer.** volume of rock to its tonnage.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Mining subsidence is
options: the rise of the surface above a mine
the downward movement/settlement of the ground surface caused by extraction of the underlying mineral
the collapse of a haul-truck tyre
the heating of coal in the goaf
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Removing support lets the overburden settle.

**Reasoning.** As underground voids close (e.g. longwall caving), the strata sag and the surface subsides, forming a subsidence trough that can damage structures, services and drainage.

**Answer.** Surface settlement from underground extraction.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: Strip mining is most suitable for
options: flat-lying, tabular near-surface deposits such as coal and lignite
alluvial placer gold deposits in river beds
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Strip mining removes overburden in long parallel strips and casts it into the adjacent mined-out void.

**Reasoning.** This geometry works only for flat-lying, tabular seams of roughly uniform thickness near the surface — typically coal and lignite.

**Answer.** Flat-lying tabular near-surface deposits (coal/lignite).

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: An airway of resistance $1.6\,\text{Ns}^2/\text{m}^8$ has its area doubled (perimeter unchanged). The new resistance is _____ Ns$^2$/m$^8$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Which pairing of parent rock → metamorphic product is correct?
options: Shale → slate and pure limestone → marble
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each protolith yields a typical metamorphic rock.

**Reasoning.** Shale → slate → phyllite → schist; pure limestone → marble; quartz sandstone → quartzite; granite → gneiss. Sandstone does not give marble, nor limestone quartzite.

**Answer.** Shale → slate; limestone → marble.

questions.subject: Mine Surveying & Geomatics
questions.stem: In a closed traverse the algebraic sum of latitudes is +0.30 m and the sum of departures is +0.40 m. The linear closing error, in m, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Closing error $=\sqrt{(\sum L)^2+(\sum D)^2}$.

**Working.**
$$e=\sqrt{0.30^2+0.40^2}=\sqrt{0.09+0.16}=\sqrt{0.25}=0.50\ \text{m}$$

**Answer.** 0.50 m.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A bed has a true dip of $30^\circ$. A vertical section is cut in a direction making $30^\circ$ with the strike. Compute the apparent dip seen in that section (degrees).

figure.caption: Apparent dip in an oblique section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,120 150,120 190,80 70,80' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='70' y1='80' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='40' y='108' font-size='8'>true dip=30 deg</text><text x='28' y='150' font-size='8'>section at 30 deg to strike</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{apparent dip})=\tan(\text{true dip})\times\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the section and the strike.

**Working.**
$$\tan\alpha=\tan30^\circ\times\sin30^\circ=0.5774\times0.5=0.2887\Rightarrow\alpha=16.1^\circ$$

**Answer.** $\approx 16.1^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Along the dip direction, a marker bed drops $50\ \text{m}$ in elevation over a horizontal distance of $100\ \text{m}$. Compute the true dip (degrees).

figure.caption: Dip from a cross-section
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 220 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='60' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='60' x2='180' y2='60' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><line x1='180' y1='60' x2='180' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 2'/><text x='80' y='52' font-size='8'>100 m</text><text x='185' y='95' font-size='8'>50 m</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\dfrac{\text{vertical drop}}{\text{horizontal distance}}\right)$.

**Working.**
$$\text{dip}=\arctan\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)=\arctan(0.5)=26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $\approx 26.6^\circ$.

questions.subject: Mine Environment & Occupational Health
questions.stem: Spontaneous-combustion risk is generally greatest in
options: thin anthracite seams with strong ventilation
thick, low-rank, high-sulphur seams worked by caving methods that leave loose coal in the goaf
fully stowed workings with no loose coal
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Loose reactive coal plus oxygen leakage drives heating.

**Reasoning.** Thick low-rank, pyritic seams mined by caving leave broken coal in the goaf where slow air leakage feeds oxidation — the classic high-risk setting; stowing or high extraction reduces the loose coal and the risk.

**Answer.** Thick low-rank high-sulphur caved seams.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: For a fixed shovel, replacing the truck fleet with fewer but larger-payload trucks generally
options: reduces the number of trucks but lengthens each loading time (more passes)
eliminates the need for a shovel
reduces the bucket fill factor to zero
always reduces total system cost regardless of haul distance
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Truck and shovel must be size-matched.

**Reasoning.** Bigger trucks need fewer units for the same tonnage, but each takes more dipper passes to fill, increasing loading time and possibly shovel waiting. An ideal match uses 3–5 passes per truck.

**Answer.** Fewer trucks but longer loading per truck.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: Limestone, rock salt and coal are examples of
options: chemically or organically formed (non-clastic) sedimentary rocks
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Non-clastic sediments form by chemical or biological processes.

**Reasoning.** Limestone precipitates/accumulates as carbonate, rock salt by evaporation of brine, and coal from accumulated plant matter — all without mechanical transport of clasts.

**Answer.** Chemical/organic sedimentary rocks.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: A rock sample of total volume $0.5\,\text{m}^3$ contains $0.1\,\text{m}^3$ of voids. Its porosity is _____ %.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Porosity $n=\dfrac{V_v}{V}$.

**Working.**
$$n=\frac{0.1}{0.5}=0.20=20\%.$$

**Answer.** $20\%$.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: A planar bed drops 50 m vertically over a horizontal distance of 100 m measured perpendicular to strike (see figure). The true dip of the bed, in degrees, is _____ (two decimals).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#b45309' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='20' y2='130' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='200' y2='130' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='80' y='148' font-size='10'>horizontal 100 m</text><text x='24' y='90' font-size='10' fill='#dc2626'>drop 50 m</text></svg>
figure.caption: Vertical drop over horizontal run

questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tan(\text{dip})=\dfrac{\text{vertical drop}}{\text{horizontal distance}}$.

**Working.**
$$\text{dip}=\arctan\!\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)=\arctan(0.5)=26.57^\circ$$

**Answer.** $26.57^\circ$.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: A bench blasthole has burden $B=3\ \text{m}$, spacing $S=4\ \text{m}$ and bench height $H=10\ \text{m}$ (figure). Compute the rock volume broken per hole ($\mathrm{m^3}$).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Volume per hole $=B\times S\times H$.

**Working.**
$$V=3\times4\times10=120\ \mathrm{m^3}$$

**Answer.** $120\ \mathrm{m^3}$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: Two parallel units (each $0.9$) are in series with a single unit of reliability $0.8$. The overall reliability is _____ . Round to three decimals.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Combine the parallel block, then the series unit.

**Working.**
$$R_{\parallel}=1-(0.1)(0.1)=0.99,\qquad R=0.99\times0.8=0.792.$$

**Answer.** $0.792$.

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: The profitability index of a project is defined as
options: present worth of future cash flows divided by the initial investment
annual profit divided by salvage value
questions.solution:
**Concept.** PI relates value created to capital spent.

**Working.** $\text{PI}=\dfrac{\text{PW of future flows}}{\text{initial investment}}$.

**Answer.** present worth of future cash flows divided by the initial investment.

questions.subject: Mining Geology
questions.stem: The smooth, curved 'conchoidal' fracture is characteristically shown by
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Fracture is breakage not controlled by cleavage.

**Reasoning.** Quartz, lacking cleavage, breaks along curved shell-like (conchoidal) surfaces, as does volcanic glass; mica, galena and calcite instead show well-developed cleavage.

**Answer.** Quartz and glass.

questions.subject: Mine Ventilation
questions.stem: A regulator (an adjustable door opening) in a ventilation split is used to
options: add resistance and so reduce/control the airflow in that split
increase the airflow without a fan
questions.solution:
**Concept.** A regulator restricts a split.

**Working.** Added resistance lowers and balances the flow.

**Answer.** add resistance to reduce/control the airflow.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Using the Obert-Duvall formula $S_p=S_1\left(0.778+0.222\frac{W}{H}\right)$ with $S_1=30\ \text{MPa}$, $W=4\ \text{m}$ and $H=2\ \text{m}$ (figure), compute the pillar strength (MPa, two decimals).

figure.caption: Pillar width and height
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='60' y='50' width='80' height='60' fill='#78716c' stroke='#475569'/><text x='92' y='130' font-size='8'>W=4</text><text x='18' y='85' font-size='8'>H=2</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** Obert-Duvall pillar strength.

**Working.**
$$S_p=30\left(0.778+0.222\times\frac{4}{2}\right)=30(0.778+0.444)=30\times1.222=36.66\ \text{MPa}$$

**Answer.** $36.66\ \text{MPa}$.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: A shearer traverses a 200 m face at a haulage (cutting) speed of 6 m/min. The time for one full cut, in minutes, is ____ (two decimals).
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Time $=\dfrac{\text{face length}}{\text{cutting speed}}$.

**Working.**
$$t=\frac{200}{6}=33.33\ \text{min}$$

**Answer.** 33.33 min.

questions.subject: Mine Economics & Planning
questions.stem: For the same activity ($t_o=2$, $t_m=5$, $t_p=8$ weeks), compute the variance of the activity duration (weeks²).

figure.caption: PERT activity
figure.markup: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><circle cx='40' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><circle cx='220' cy='55' r='14' fill='#e0f2fe' stroke='#475569'/><line x1='54' y1='55' x2='204' y2='55' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#pe)'/><defs><marker id='pe' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0 0 L6 3 L0 6 Z' fill='#475569'/></marker></defs><text x='130' y='42' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>to=2, tm=5, tp=8</text></svg>

questions.solution:
**Concept.** PERT activity variance is $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$.

**Working.**
$$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{8-2}{6}\right)^2 = (1)^2 = 1$$

**Answer.** Variance $= \mathbf{1}$ week².

questions.subject: Mine Economics, Planning & Systems Engineering
questions.stem: With $\lambda=6$/hr and $\mu=10$/hr, the average time spent in the system $W_s$ is _____ hours.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $W_s=\dfrac{1}{\mu-\lambda}$.

**Working.**
$$W_s=\frac{1}{10-6}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25.$$

**Answer.** $0.25$ hour.

questions.subject: Rock Mechanics
questions.stem: For $\sigma_x=50$, $\sigma_y=10$, $\tau_{xy}=15\,\text{MPa}$, the maximum in-plane shear stress is _____ MPa.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** $\tau_{max}=R$ (radius of the Mohr circle).

**Working.**
$$\tau_{max}=25.$$

**Answer.** $25\,\text{MPa}$.

questions.subject: Surface Mining
questions.stem: A bucket wheel excavator has 12 buckets on the wheel, which rotates at 4 rev/min. The number of bucket discharges per minute is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Discharges/min $=$ buckets on wheel $\times$ wheel speed (rev/min).

**Working.**
$$d=12\times4=48\ \text{discharges/min}$$

**Answer.** 48.

questions.subject: Drilling & Blasting
questions.stem: Explosive A has a weight strength of 1000 and explosive B has 900 (same units). The relative weight strength of B with respect to A, in %, is ____.
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Relative weight strength $=\dfrac{\text{strength of B}}{\text{strength of A}}\times100$.

**Working.**
$$RWS=\frac{900}{1000}\times100=90\%$$

**Answer.** 90%.

questions.subject: Underground Mining
questions.stem: Room-and-pillar mining of a metalliferous deposit is most appropriate for
options: flat-lying or gently dipping tabular deposits at moderate depth, with pillars left for roof support
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Room-and-pillar suits bedded, low-dip deposits.

**Reasoning.** Rooms are mined leaving regular pillars to hold the roof; the geometry works for flat/gently dipping tabular orebodies (e.g. limestone, salt, some base metals) where depth/stress allow economic pillar sizes.

**Answer.** Flat-lying tabular deposits at moderate depth.
